channel.title: R on Daniels page
channel.description: Recent content in R on Daniels page

item.title: Using if else with pipes
item.description: A nice way to use if else within pipes is (see here): library(magrittr) runif(1) %>% magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% {if(.) "foo" else "bar"} ## [1] "bar" Alternatively, the when function from the purrr package can be used: library(purrr, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE) runif(1) %>% purrr::when(. <= 0.5 ~ "foo", . > 0.5 ~ "bar") ## [1] "bar" A nice way to use a vector of inputs is provided by the case_when function from dplyr together with mutate or transmute:

item.title: Using if else with pipes
item.description: A nice way to use if else within pipes is (see here): library(magrittr) runif(1) %>% magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% {if(.) "foo" else "bar"} ## [1] "bar" Alternatively, the when function from the purrr package can be used: library(purrr, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE) runif(1) %>% purrr::when(. <= 0.5 ~ "foo", . > 0.5 ~ "bar") ## [1] "bar" A nice way to use a vector of inputs is provided by the case_when function from dplyr together with mutate or transmute:

item.title: Feedback with tribbles
item.description: The ordering and display of tables in Rmarkdown (aka data.frames or tibbles) has become so good. I actually prefer it over using print right now. Especially, because I can format in in an intuitive way by using the tribble() function. Here is an example. Let us say I want to summarize the basic set up for an optimization. Some dummy variables might look like this: library(tibble) number_iterations <- 1000 stoping_criterion <- -1 stepsize <- 0.

item.title: More tidy evaluation
item.description: The first post is more explaatory and can be found here. This one is more a note-taking effort and hence much more be spartan. The main goal is to go through some of the functionality of the rlang package. Basics rlang has multiple constructor-functions that can be used instead of the base Rcombine operator c(). These are: lgl(...), int(...), dbl(...), cpl(...), chr(..., .encoding = NULL), bytes(...) and ll(...). The idea here is that this way the code is more explicit than using c() (The resulting data can be inferred from the functions).

item.title: First Steps with Tydy Evaluation
item.description: ‘Multiple exclamation marks,’ he went on, shaking his head, ‘are a sure sign of a diseased mind.’ - Eric, Terry Pratchett The purpose of this post is to start a protocol my process for getting used to tidy evaluation. My actual course of action was a bit more unstructured than the one presented here. But not by much I wanted to preserve at least some degree of sanity for eventual re-reads.
